serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mutton cutlet is one piece weighing approximately 80–100 g, commonly served as a snack or side dish at mamak stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Cutlet — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Cutlet (Indian-style breaded and deep-fried). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style mutton cutlet. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous breaded and fried meat patty/cutlet entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification scale. The breadcrumb coating is the primary glycemic contributor; carbohydrate content is low at 8.59g/100g. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (8.59g × 0.9 serving = 7.73g) / 100 = 4.25, rounded to 4.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 558 mg/100g and high saturated fat 7.8 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit mutton cutlet due to its high sodium content (558 mg/100g, approaching the 600 mg/100g threshold) and elevated saturated fat, which together contribute to raised blood pressure and arterial stiffness; if consumed, portion size should be limited to one small piece and high-sodium condiments must be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.59 g/100g) but high fat and low-boundary GI from breadcrumb coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton cutlet has a low carbohydrate load (8.59 g/100g, sugar 0.39 g/100g) and high protein (22.06 g/100g) which supports glycaemic stability, but the high total fat (19.42 g/100g) and deep-frying method increase caloric density and insulin resistance risk; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, request baked or grilled preparation, and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (7.8 g/100g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100g from deep-frying), and high sodium (558 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mutton cutlet is high-risk for cardiovascular disease patients due to its elevated saturated fat (7.8 g/100g), estimated trans fat from deep-frying (~0.2 g/100g), and high sodium (558 mg/100g), all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol, arterial inflammation, and blood pressure; this food should be avoided or consumed only very occasionally in small portions as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid), saturated fat >5 g/100g, trans fat from frying, and pro-inflammatory AGEs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton cutlet is strongly contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to red meat arachidonic acid content, saturated fat exceeding the 5 g/100g RA threshold, trans fat from deep-frying, and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated by high-heat frying, all of which promote systemic inflammation and joint damage; patients should avoid this food and substitute with baked or grilled lean poultry or fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton cutlet is a popular Indian-Singaporean snack and side dish commonly found at mamak stalls and Indian Muslim eateries across Singapore. It is prepared by mincing or finely chopping mutton (goat or lamb), seasoning it with a blend of aromatic spices including ginger, garlic, chilli, cumin, and coriander, shaping the mixture into oval or round patties, coating them in beaten egg and breadcrumbs, and deep-frying until golden brown and crispy. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and a dense, spiced, savoury interior. Per 100g, mutton cutlet provides 297 kcal, 22.06g protein, 19.42g fat (including approximately 7.8g saturated fat and an estimated 0.2g trans fat from deep-frying), 8.59g carbohydrate, 0.39g sugar, 1.52g dietary fibre, and 558mg sodium. The high protein content makes it nutritionally dense, but the deep-frying method, saturated fat level, and sodium content raise concerns for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI category per University of Sydney classification) based on analogous breaded fried meat products, with a low glycemic load per serving (~4.2) due to the modest carbohydrate content. Mutton cutlet is halal-certified in most mamak contexts and is commonly served as a breakfast item, snack, or accompaniment to rice or bread. It is a culturally significant dish in Singapore's mamak food heritage.

Is mutton cutlet suitable for diabetics?
What is the protein content of mutton cutlet?
Is mutton cutlet high in sodium?
Can I eat mutton cutlet if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of mutton cutlet?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a confidence of 0.95 for an exact match to Indian-style breaded and deep-fried mutton cutlet. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from standard mutton fat composition and deep-frying literature where not explicitly reported by HPB.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific mutton cutlet preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for mutton cutlet. trans_fat_g set to 0.2 based on estimation from deep-frying literature for breaded meat products; HPB does not explicitly report trans fat for this item. saturated_fat_g of 7.8g/100g is estimated from standard mutton fat composition data consistent with HPB reporting conventions. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on breaded fried meat patty entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Indian-style mutton cutlet was found in approved sources. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low: ≤55; medium: 56–69; high: ≥70); gi_category is therefore correctly set to 'low'. calcium_mg (26.0) and iron_mg (8.38) are sourced directly from HPB Foodo.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced from input JSON.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per G5 requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with glycemic_index 55. Per University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 = low, GI 56–69 = medium, GI ≥70 = high. A GI of exactly 55 is correctly categorised as 'low'. Updated gi_source notes and llm_training description to reflect this correction. Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reference 'low-boundary GI' for accuracy.
